1: Chapter 23 returns to the two-sisters allegory. Unlike the Ezekiel 16 foundling allegory that focused on Jerusalem alone, Ezekiel 23 presents both kingdoms — Israel (northern, Samaria) and Judah (southern, Jerusalem) — as co-wives of YHWH whose careers of political prostitution are traced in parallel. The allegory is more explicitly political than Ezekiel 16: the "lovers" are identified by national name rather than left as general partners.
2: Two women, daughters of one mother: Both Samaria and Jerusalem share the same covenantal origin — both are daughters of the original covenant people formed at Sinai. The shared mother grounds the comparison: these are not different national identities but two branches of the same covenant community. The parallel histories of apostasy that follow implicate the unified origin in the shared guilt.
3: Egypt as origin of the prostitution: The sexual activity began in Egypt — "their breasts were squeezed there and their virgin nipples fondled." This startling claim grounds the political prostitution in the Exodus era: Israel's fundamental identity was formed in Egypt, and Egypt was the first arena of corrupt political-religious compromise. Historically, the pre-Sinai period includes the Israelites' worship of Egyptian gods (Joshua 24:14; Ezekiel 20:7-8) and Solomon's Egyptian marriages (1 Kings 3:1; Ezekiel 11:1-8).
4: The sisters' names: Oholah ('her tent/tabernacle') and Oholibah ('my tent is in her'). The names are etymologically significant: Samaria (Oholah) has her own tent — a sanctuary that belongs to herself, not to YHWH (the Bethel/Dan bull sanctuaries of Jeroboam I, 1 Kings 12:28-29). Jerusalem (Oholibah) has YHWH's tent within her — the temple. The names encode the theological verdict: Samaria had an unauthorized cult from her founding; Jerusalem had a legitimate cult that she betrayed.
5: Oholah (Samaria) and Assyria: The northern kingdom's entanglement with Assyria began with Menahem's tribute to Tiglath-Pileser III (745-727 BCE) — 1 Kings 15:19-20: a thousand talents of silver to secure Assyrian backing for his throne. Pekah's anti-Assyrian coalition and Hosea's submission followed. The "Assyrians as lovers" metaphor in Ezekiel captures the political reality: Israel bought Assyrian favor with tribute, becoming economically and politically dependent on the empire that would eventually destroy it.
6: Assyrian warriors in their military glory: Ezekiel's description of the Assyrian warriors — clothed in purple, governors and commanders, horsemen, desirable young men — reflects accurate knowledge of Assyrian military display. Neo-Assyrian reliefs (the Nineveh palace reliefs now in the British Museum) show exactly this: officials in purple robes, cavalry units with ornamental gear, hierarchical military organization. The "desirable" quality of the Assyrian military machine was its disciplined power, which smaller states both feared and sought to harness through alliance.
7: Oholah gave herself to "the choice of all the Assyrians" — the elite of the empire. The political alliances Samaria made were with the highest levels of Assyrian power, not minor border officials. Kol-gilulei Assyria ('all the idols of Assyria') — the political prostitution was inseparable from the religious: accepting the Assyrian political order meant accepting the Assyrian gods (2 Kings 16:10-16; Ahaz adopted the Damascene altar after meeting Tiglath-Pileser).
8: The Egyptian "prostitution" continued: Oholah (Samaria) never abandoned the Egypt-rooted apostasy from her beginnings. The northern kingdom maintained religious forms that had Egyptian associations (bull-calf iconography at Bethel and Dan echoes the Egyptian Apis bull and Canaanite El-bull imagery). The continued Egypt-prostitution alongside the Assyrian alliance represents the layering of successive apostasies without abandonment of earlier ones.
9: Handed over to her Assyrian lovers: The historical event: the Assyrian destruction of Samaria in 722 BCE under Sargon II, following the siege begun by Shalmaneser V. 2 Kings 17:3-6 records that Hoshea, king of Israel, submitted to Shalmaneser but then sent messengers to Egypt and withheld tribute — the same Egypt-over-Assyria shift that Ezekiel 23:7-8 describes. The people were deported to Halah, Habor, the Gozan River, and the cities of the Medes — scattered across the Assyrian empire.
10: The execution of Oholah: Gallu ba — those who executed her were her own lovers, the Assyrians. The typical Assyrian punishment for a rebellious vassal included stripping (seizure of wealth), public humiliation, and execution of the leadership. Veyhi shem le'nashim ('she became a name/byword among women'): the northern kingdom's fate became the cautionary example that prophets like Hosea used. That Ezekiel's Judean audience knew this example and still repeated it is the charge of Ezekiel 23:11.
11: "Her sister Oholibah saw this... and was more corrupt": The logic of the comparison is devastating: Jerusalem witnessed Samaria's destruction by Assyria and still repeated the same pattern — and exceeded it. Historically, the fall of the northern kingdom in 722 BCE was within living memory of Ezekiel's generation (a century). The prophets Micah and Isaiah used the fall of Samaria as a warning to Judah in exactly this way (Micah 1:6-7; Isaiah 28:1-4).
12: Oholibah lusted after Assyria too: Judah's Assyrian entanglements: Ahaz's appeal to Tiglath-Pileser III against the Aram-Ephraim coalition (2 Kings 16:7-9) — paying tribute from the temple treasury. Ahaz then visited Damascus to meet Tiglath-Pileser and adopted an Assyrian altar design for the Jerusalem temple (2 Kings 16:10-16). Isaiah condemned this as reliance on human power over YHWH (Isa 7-8; 30-31). The governors and commanders, the horsemen — the same imagery as Ezekiel 23:6.
13: YHWH's declaration: Va'ereh ki nit'ame shetayyhem ('I saw that both were defiled'). The divine observation confirms the parallel guilt. Despite witnessing Oholah's destruction, Oholibah followed the same pattern — the witness-experience that should have produced repentance instead stimulated imitation. The moral psychology here is realistic: it is easier to continue familiar patterns than to be changed by observing another's judgment, especially when the judgment seems remote.
14: The Babylonian murals: Vatosif el-ta'avoteha vatere anshei mequtteim al-haqir tzalmei Kasdim chaquqim basheshar ('she increased her prostitution and she saw men carved on the wall, images of Chaldeans carved in vermilion/red ochre'). This is a remarkable historical detail. Neo-Babylonian palace decoration featured large-scale relief carvings and painted murals of military figures, courtiers, and processions — visible to any visitor to Babylon's administrative buildings. Ezekiel and his fellow exiles had firsthand exposure to these murals in Babylon.
15: Belted turbans and flowing robes: The detailed description of the Babylonian figures — chaluzei meznachim al-motnehem yeru'ot tvolet al-rasheihem ('girded with belts at their waists, with flowing turbans on their heads') — matches Neo-Babylonian artistic conventions. Cylinder seals and relief carvings from the 6th century BCE show Babylonian officials in exactly this attire: wrapped sashes, tall turbans or tiered headdresses, long robes. The visual specificity suggests Ezekiel (or his source) had direct knowledge of Babylonian visual culture, consistent with his exile location.
16: Desire at first sight: Vatit'aveh alehem lema'ereh eineiha ('she lusted after them at the sight of her eyes'): the visual stimulus triggering the political-religious desire. The sending of messengers (vatishlach malakhim alehem Kasdimah) = the diplomatic overtures from Jerusalem to Babylon. Historical referent: Hezekiah's hospitality to the Babylonian envoys of Merodach-Baladan (2 Kings 20:12-15; Isaiah 39:1-4) — an early Babylonian alliance that Isaiah condemned as the first step toward the ultimate Babylonian captivity.
17: The Babylonians coming to her bed: The Chaldean-lust allegory covers multiple phases of Judah-Babylonian relations: the Hezekiah-Merodach-Baladan contact (705 BCE), Manasseh's submission to Babylon (2 Chronicles 33:11), the complex vassalage of Jehoiakim and Zedekiah under Nebuchadnezzar (605-587 BCE). Each phase is a successive "coming to bed." Vatitma bam ('she was defiled by them'): the defilement comes precisely through the alliances that were supposed to provide security.
18: The alienation of YHWH: Vatiqal nafshi me'aleiha ('my soul recoiled from her'): YHWH's emotional withdrawal from Jerusalem mirrors the estrangement in 16:15 and the departure of the glory in Ezekiel 10-11. The word qal nafshi (my soul was alienated/repulsed) uses the same root as qalas (to be light, contemptible). As Oholah had been handed over to Assyria, so Oholibah will be handed over to those from whom she has now turned away.
19: Increased prostitution recalling Egyptian youth: Oholibah's third phase: returning to Egypt-desire after the Babylonian phase. This mirrors Zedekiah's pro-Egyptian policy — reaching out to Egypt for help against Babylon (2 Kings 24:20; Jeremiah 37:5-8). The "days of her youth in Egypt" refers to the same Egyptian-origin apostasy introduced in Ezekiel 23:3. The cycle returns to its beginning, but with greater intensity. Each successive alliance does not replace but compounds the previous ones.
20: Egypt's explicit description: Asher basar chamorim besaram ('whose flesh was like donkey flesh'): the most sexually explicit line in Ezekiel, equating Egypt's sexual attraction to bestiality. The political commentary is buried in the biology: Egypt's appeal was raw, animal power with no covenantal quality — pure imperial bulk without relational loyalty. Egypt's military as a "great flow of water" (zirmat susim zimratam) was the fantasy of military might that Israel imagined would protect them but never did (Isaiah 30:7: "Egypt's help is worthless and empty").
21: The return to Egyptian desire is described as 'reviving the lewdness of your youth' — the circle of apostasy is complete. Ezekiel frames Israel's history as an extended return to the starting point: everything that happened between Sinai and Zedekiah was a variation on the original failure to abandon Egyptian religious-political habits. The final appeal to Egypt is not a new departure but a regression to the oldest pattern.
22: The lovers turned enemies: The theological principle underlying the judgment: the lovers (alliances) whom Oholibah used will be turned against her. YHWH brings up her former allies (mesanim alayich) as the instruments of judgment. The former ally becomes the instrument of divine judgment: Babylon, which Jerusalem sought as a lover, returns as her executioner. This is the inner logic of covenant-idolatry — the power trusted in place of YHWH becomes the mechanism of punishment.
23: The Babylonian coalition: Pqod uShoa uQoa — possibly specific peoples or districts of the Babylonian empire. Kol-beney Ashshur (all the sons of Assyria): Assyrian military units incorporated into the Babylonian imperial force. The multinational character of the Babylonian army (Jeremiah 52:28-30 mentions various national components) made it the representative instrument of international judgment — 'all the nations' against Jerusalem.
24: Battle equipment and judicial execution: Ve'ata alayich choshen reken ('they will come against you with weapons, chariots, wagons'): the full military equipment of the Neo-Babylonian army. The juridical element — veneshpatu otach kemishpatam ('they will judge you according to their judgments') — is crucial: the Babylonians' punishment of Jerusalem is simultaneously YHWH's judicial verdict executed through human agents. The pagan army becomes YHWH's court officers.
25: Divine jealousy and the removal of nose and ears: Vehishteta ki ani YHWH ('you will know that I am YHWH'). The mutilation — vesaru appeikh ve'oznayich ('they will take your nose and your ears') — reflects actual ancient Near Eastern punishments for adultery. Middle Assyrian Laws (tablet A, §4-5, §15) prescribed nose-cutting for a woman caught in adultery. The judicial punishment fits the allegorical crime. The removal of the ornaments (Ezekiel 16:11-13) was the first stripping; the removal of nose and ears is the ultimate deforming punishment.
26: The stripping of garments and jewelry echoes the reversal of the bridal adornment in Ezekiel 16:39. Everything YHWH gave in the betrothal-lavishing is removed in the punishment-stripping. The divine gifts — embroidered cloth, jewelry, ornaments — are taken away by the very forces Jerusalem courted in place of YHWH. The lovers strip the bride who betrayed the husband.
27: The end of the prostitution and the Egyptian connection: Vehishbatti zimmateich mimennech ('I will put an end to your lewdness from you'). The purpose of the judgment is explicitly therapeutic/remedial: the punishment ends the behavior. Velo tissi einayich alehem veMitzrayim lo tizkerevat od ('and you will not lift your eyes to them, and Egypt you will remember no more'): the Egyptian desire that has persisted since the beginning (Ezekiel 23:3) will finally end. The judgment accomplishes what repentance should have achieved voluntarily.
28: The principle of judgment through the rejected: YHWH delivers Jerusalem into the hand of those she hates — the enemies created by the withdrawal of the alliances. Historical reality: when Zedekiah's rebellion against Babylon failed, Egypt withdrew rather than fight to the finish (Jeremiah 37:7-8). The "lovers" — Babylonian and Egyptian — both became hostile; the two great powers Jerusalem tried to play against each other both turned against her.
29: The full stripping: Ve'asu otach be'sinah ('they will deal with you in hatred'): the former lovers act with active malice. Velaqachu kol yegia'ech ('they will take all your earnings/property'): the stripping is also an economic stripping — the accumulated wealth of Jerusalem becomes war plunder. The nakedness and exposure follow the same logic as Ezekiel 16:37-41: the very partners in the apostasy execute the punishment, which is poetically just.
30: The summary verdict: Asher asit etkeh ('these things are done to you'). The explicit causal chain: Jerusalem did these things; therefore these things happen to her. The moral coherence of the judgment is insisted upon: this is not random catastrophe but the proportionate consequence of documented infidelity. The nations she went to (asher zagit achar ha-goyim) are the nations through whom she is judged.
31: The cup passed from Oholah to Oholibah: Bederekh achoteikh halacht ('you walked in the way of your sister'): the imitation principle. The cup of Samaria — the Assyrian destruction — was Oholibah's warning and example; instead of avoiding it, Oholibah drank the same cup. The cup of judgment (kos achoteikh) passes between the sisters. The cup-of-wrath imagery (Isaiah 51:17-22; Jeremiah 25:15-29; Habakkuk 2:16) is a common prophetic trope for divine judgment.
32: The cup of Samaria: deep and wide: The cup's dimensions — deep and wide — indicate comprehensiveness of judgment. Tihyeh litsechok ulelag ('it will hold much laughter and mockery'): the cup provokes mockery from observers who watch the judgment unfold. The surrounding nations who were sympathetic to Samaria's claims will watch Jerusalem's fall with the same contemptuous mockery (Lamentations 1:7; Ezekiel 2:15-16).
33: Intoxication of devastation: Shikaron vetsuqah ('drunkenness and desolation'): the double effect of the cup — drunken incapacity and devastation. The combination captures the complete disorientation and ruin of the city that must drink the full cup of judgment. The cup of Samaria's sister is identical in effect though delivered through a different imperial power.
34: The full consumption of the cup — tishetihu vetimtzehu ('you will drink it and drain it') — and the gnawing of the potsherds: the complete consumption and even the residue scraped from the vessel. Nothing of the judgment is spared; the cup is emptied completely. The tearing of one's own breasts (veshodayich tigenami) — extreme grief expressed through self-harm — is the ultimate expression of the cup's effect.
35: The verdict: "You have forgotten me": The theological summary: ya'an asher shakachti oti ('because you forgot me'). The root failure is forgetting YHWH — the same diagnosis as Ezekiel 16:22. Vetishlichi oti acharei gavech ('and cast me behind your back'): the body-language metaphor of dismissal — turning one's back on YHWH. The judgment follows as the direct consequence: vegam at se'i zimmateich vetaz nuteich ('you yourself bear your lewdness and your prostitution').
36: YHWH calls Ezekiel to judge both sisters jointly (hatishpot et-Oholah ve'et-Oholibah): the combined indictment of Israel and Judah. The shared guilt is named: the blood on their hands and the idols in their houses. The joint accusation is a theological statement: despite the separate histories and separate divine acts of judgment (Assyria destroyed Samaria; Babylon destroyed Jerusalem), both kingdoms share equally in the guilt of covenant apostasy.
37: Child sacrifice specified: Both sisters committed adultery with idols and sacrificed their children born to YHWH. The child sacrifice charge from Ezekiel 16:20-21 is repeated here for the northern kingdom as well: this was not exclusively a southern sin. The archaeological evidence from the Tophet at Carthage (a Phoenician/Canaanite site) confirms child sacrifice practices across the wider Canaanite cultural sphere, suggesting the Israelite apostasy adopted real regional practices.
38: Temple defilement and Sabbath profanation: The two-fold defilement — the sanctuary defiled (timme'u et-miqdashi) and the Sabbaths profaned (ve'et-Shabbetotai chillelu) — on the same day as child sacrifice. The juxtaposition is horrifying: bringing blood-stained hands directly into the sanctuary for worship. This is the exact situation Isaiah condemns in Isaiah 1:10-15: 'I cannot bear your evil assemblies... stop bringing meaningless offerings; your incense is detestable to me... when you spread out your hands in prayer, I hide my eyes from you; even when you offer many prayers, I am not listening. Your hands are full of blood.'
39: The desecration sequence: children killed in the morning, then the parents coming to the temple. The temporal sequence makes the horror explicit — the same day, the same hands, the same people moving between Molech and YHWH. The behavioral pattern that Jeremiah identified as 'entering this house' after doing the things YHWH hates (Jeremiah 7:9-11) is here described in its most extreme form.
40: Sending for men from far away: The summons to the nations as lovers. Ve'af ki shelachten la'anashim ba'im memerchaq ('and even to men coming from afar you sent a messenger'): the diplomatic overtures to distant powers. The bathing, painting eyes (vatakahal einayich — kohl/eye makeup), and adornment for the visitor parallels the female preparation for a paramour — the diplomatic hospitality as sexual preparation. This is an accurate description of the diplomatic reception parties that characterized ancient Near Eastern statecraft.
41: The couch and the prepared table: Veyashavt al-mita kevuda ushulchan aruch lefaneiha ('you sat on an honorable couch with a table spread before it'): the formal diplomatic banquet setting. The incense and oil from YHWH's provision used in the diplomatic reception for foreign emissaries — the covenantal gifts redirected to impress potential political allies. The 'table' imagery anticipates the critique of 39:20 and 44:16 where YHWH's own table (the altar) is the proper context for these goods.
42: Noise of carefree crowd: The crowd with the women (qa-kol hamon shalevim bah) represents the diplomatic parties and the festive atmosphere of the court-prostitution. Uvasabba'im me'imidbar itam tzamidim el-yedeihen ('with drunkards brought from the desert, they put bracelets on their hands'): the reveling mixed crowd receiving jewelry — the bridal gifts (Ezekiel 16:11-13) distributed to foreign visitors. The transformation of the sacred marriage gifts into diplomatic favors.
43: The divine rhetorical question: Ha'am lezi'nah titzneh hu — the exhausted and worn-out woman continuing to prostitute herself. The image is of a court that has exhausted itself through successive alliances yet continues to pursue more. The political-theological exhaustion of Jerusalem under its last kings — the rapid succession of alliances and betrayals under Jehoiakim and Zedekiah — is captured in the image of the worn-out prostitute still soliciting.
44: The lovers going in to the prostitute as one goes to a loose woman — the contempt of the nations for Jerusalem, which has made herself universally available. The comparison between the temple-prostitute (if reading zonah as cult prostitute) and Jerusalem is the point: Jerusalem has made herself like the very worst category of religious-sexual practice she was supposed to transcend.
45: Righteous men as judges: Ve'anashim tzaddiqim hemah yishpetu otam mishpat no'afot umishpat shofechot dam ('righteous men — they will judge them with the judgment of adulteresses and with the judgment of women who shed blood'): the legal category. The "righteous men" are the instruments of the judicial community exercising the covenant law. The punishment for adultery and child murder is named without specification — the law was clear (Leviticus 20:2, 10) and both crimes carried capital sentences.
46: Mob assembly: Ki koh amar Adonai YHWH ha'al aleichem qahal ('for thus says the Lord YHWH: bring up against them an assembly'): the qahal (assembly/mob) as executioner. The legal execution by stoning involved community participation; here the 'assembly' is the invading Babylonian forces serving as executors of the communal death penalty. The warfare is juridical in Ezekiel's framing: Babylon is the divine court's enforcement mechanism, not merely a conquering empire.
47: Stoning and sword together: the assembly executes the verdict with both the traditional communal (stoning) and military (sword) methods. The killing of sons and daughters (beneihem uvnoteihem yaharegu) — the covenant-curse reversal: the children who were sacrificed (Ezekiel 23:37) are now joined in death by those who survive the initial assault. The houses burned: the complete destruction of property accompanying the judicial execution.
48: The instructive purpose of judgment: Lenashim mussar ('a warning to women') — the judgment of the two sisters is explicitly pedagogical. The surrounding nations who observe Jerusalem's punishment will receive the instruction that covenant apostasy leads to such ends. The public nature of the judgment is integral to its purpose: YHWH's judicial actions are transparent, comprehensible, and serve as moral education for all who witness them.
49: Bearing the guilt of idolatry: Venasatu zimmateichem aleyhen ('you will bear the guilt of your idol-worship upon yourselves'): individual and corporate accountability stated as the final principle. Vidatetem ki Ani Adonai YHWH: the recognition formula closes the extended double-allegory. The entire chapter builds toward this: the goal of the comprehensive judgment on both sisters is that they will know — finally, through the enacted consequences — that YHWH alone is God.
